title: Fractions
blurb: Equivalent fractions, comparing, the four operations, and word problems — taught with bars you can split, merge, and combine.

lessons.title: Equivalent Fractions
lessons.concreteComparison: Half a pizza is half a pizza — whether you cut it into **2 big slices** and take 1, or **6 small slices** and take 3. $\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{6}$: different numbers, the exact same amount. Fractions like these are **equivalent**.
lessons.objective: Build equivalent fractions by multiplying the top and bottom by the same number, and reduce a fraction to simplest form by dividing.
concept: Splitting **every** piece of a fraction bar into the same number of smaller pieces multiplies the top and bottom by that number — and the shaded amount never changes. So $\frac{1}{2}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{3}{6}$ are all the same amount.
This is the same move as **equivalent ratios**: multiply both numbers by the same number and the value stays the same.
**Simplest form** runs it backwards: divide the top and bottom by the same number until nothing divides both. It is the equivalent fraction with the smallest whole numbers.
lessons.keyIdea: Multiply or divide the top and bottom by the **same** number and a fraction's value never changes. Simplest form is the version with the smallest whole numbers.

facts.title: Equivalent fractions
facts.explanation: Multiply top and bottom by the same number n — the amount is unchanged.

workedExamples.statement: Write a fraction equal to $\frac{2}{3}$ that has **6** on the bottom.
workedExamples.note: Find what 3 was multiplied by, then do the same on top.
workedExamples.solution: $6 \div 3 = 2$, so multiply top and bottom by 2: $\frac{2}{3} = \frac{4}{6}$.

workedExamples.note: Find a number that divides both, and keep going until none does.
workedExamples.solution: Both divide by 4: $\frac{8}{12} = \frac{2}{3}$. Nothing divides 2 and 3, so $\frac{2}{3}$ is simplest.

commonMistakes.text: Adding the same number to top and bottom (e.g. $\frac{1}{2}$ → $\frac{2}{3}$).
commonMistakes.correction: You must **multiply** (or divide), not add — $\frac{1}{2}=\frac{2}{4}$, not $\frac{2}{3}$.

commonMistakes.text: Stopping before simplest form (e.g. simplifying $\frac{8}{12}$ only to $\frac{4}{6}$).
commonMistakes.correction: Keep dividing until no number divides both top and bottom.

tryIt.statement: Write a fraction equal to $\frac{3}{4}$ with 12 on the bottom.

steps.title: Same amount, more pieces
steps.teach: This bar shows $\frac{1}{2}$. Tap **+** to split every piece into more — watch the orange amount stay exactly the same while the numbers grow.

steps.title: Why the amount doesn't change
beats: Splitting **every** piece into the same number multiplies the **top and bottom by that same number**.
$\frac{1}{2}$ split into 2 becomes $\frac{2}{4}$; into 3, $\frac{3}{6}$ — the orange amount never moved.
Fractions that shade the same amount are **equivalent fractions**.
Remember **equivalent ratios**? Exact same move — multiply both numbers by the same number.

steps.title: Building an equivalent fraction
steps.intro: Find what the bottom was multiplied by, then do the same to the top.

examples.prompt: Write a fraction equal to $\frac{2}{3}$ with **6** on the bottom.
steps: $6 \div 3 = 2$, so multiply top and bottom by 2.
Multiply top and bottom of $\frac{3}{4}$ by 3: $\frac{9}{12}$.

steps.title: Spot the equivalent fraction
steps.intro: Multiply top and bottom by the same number. Tap the match.

steps.title: Multiply top and bottom
steps.prompt: Multiply the top and bottom of $\frac{3}{4}$ by 5. What do you get?

steps.title: Music runs on equivalent fractions
steps.body: A half note, two quarter notes, four eighth notes — all the same length of time, written as $\frac{1}{2}=\frac{2}{4}=\frac{4}{8}$. Musicians read equivalent fractions every single bar.

steps.title: Simplest form: the biggest pieces
steps.teach: Same idea, backwards. Tap **Merge** to divide the top and bottom by the same number until you can't anymore — that's **simplest form**.

steps.title: Dividing down to simplest form
beats: **Simplest form** is the equivalent fraction with the **fewest, biggest pieces** — the smallest numbers.
To get there, **divide** the top and bottom by the same number until nothing divides both.
$\frac{8}{12}$: both divide by 4 → $\frac{2}{3}$. Nothing divides 2 and 3, so you're done.
Dividing top and bottom by the same number is just splitting in reverse — the amount stays the same.

steps.title: Reducing to simplest form
steps.intro: Divide top and bottom by a common number, and keep going until none is left.

steps.title: Reduce to simplest form
steps.intro: Divide top and bottom until nothing divides both. Tap the simplest form.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Equivalent fractions** shade the same amount: multiply the top and bottom by the **same** number.
It's the same move as **equivalent ratios** — both numbers grow together.
**Simplest form** divides the top and bottom by the same number until nothing divides both.
Never add to the top and bottom — that changes the amount. Only multiply or divide.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Two moves to lock in: building an equal fraction (multiply) and reducing one (divide). Run this mixed set — tap a wrong choice and just try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.explanation: $\frac{5}{10}$ and $\frac{6}{12}$ both reduce to $\frac{1}{2}$, but $\frac{4}{9}$ does not.

steps.title: You've finished "Equivalent Fractions"
steps.body: You can now grow a fraction into an equal one and shrink any fraction to simplest form — the two moves behind almost everything else with fractions. Next up: **comparing fractions**, where you give two fractions the same-size pieces and just compare.

lessons.title: Comparing Fractions
lessons.concreteComparison: Two friends each get their own pie — Pie A is cut into **3 equal slices** and you take 2; Pie B is cut into **5 equal slices** and your friend takes 3. $\frac{2}{3}$ vs $\frac{3}{5}$: who got more? The slices are different sizes, so you can't just count them. **Give both pies the same number of slices first** — then you simply count who has more shaded pieces. This is the one idea behind every fraction comparison.
lessons.objective: Compare two fractions by rewriting them with a common denominator, and order three or more fractions from smallest to largest.
concept: To compare $\frac{2}{3}$ and $\frac{3}{5}$, **give them the same-size pieces** (common denominator). $\frac{2}{3}=\frac{10}{15}$ and $\frac{3}{5}=\frac{9}{15}$. Now both bars have 15 pieces and you just count: 10 shaded beats 9 shaded, so $\frac{2}{3}>\frac{3}{5}$.

This is the **fraction twin of Ratios** — "make the water equal" from *Comparing Ratios* is the same move: get both on the same scale, then compare. And it uses **Lesson 1** (Equivalent Fractions): multiply the top and bottom by the same number to reach the common denominator.

**Ordering** three fractions and **spotting equal ones** work the same way — convert every fraction to the same denominator, then read off the order of the numerators. If two numerators match, the fractions are equal.
lessons.keyIdea: **Give fractions the same-size pieces (common denominator), then compare numerators.** The fraction with more shaded pieces is larger.

facts.title: Comparing with a common denominator
facts.latex: If $\dfrac{a}{n}$ and $\dfrac{b}{n}$ have the **same denominator**, then $\dfrac{a}{n} > \dfrac{b}{n}$ when $a > b$.
facts.explanation: Find a common denominator by multiplying (or using the LCM), convert both fractions, then compare the numerators.

facts.title: Equal fractions
facts.explanation: Two fractions are equal when their cross-products match — which is just another way of saying they have the same value after finding a common denominator.

workedExamples.note: Find a common denominator (the product $3 \times 5 = 15$ always works), convert both, then compare.
Convert $\frac{2}{3}$ to sixths and compare, or use equivalent fractions from Lesson 1.
workedExamples.solution: $\frac{2}{3} = \frac{4}{6}$ (multiply top and bottom by 2). Yes — they are equal.

workedExamples.statement: Order $\frac{1}{2}$, $\frac{2}{3}$, and $\frac{3}{8}$ from smallest to largest.
workedExamples.note: Use the common denominator 24 (or the LCM of 2, 3, and 8 = 24).

commonMistakes.text: Comparing numerators before finding a common denominator — e.g. saying $\frac{3}{5} > \frac{2}{3}$ because $3 > 2$.
commonMistakes.correction: The pieces are different sizes until you get a common denominator. $\frac{2}{3}=\frac{10}{15}$ vs $\frac{3}{5}=\frac{9}{15}$, so $\frac{2}{3}$ wins.
A bigger denominator means **smaller** pieces, not a bigger fraction. Always convert first.

commonMistakes.text: Stopping at the common denominator without converting the numerators.
commonMistakes.correction: Whatever you multiply the bottom by, multiply the top by the **same** number. Both parts must change.

tryIt.solution: $\frac{3}{4}=\frac{9}{12}$ (multiply top and bottom by 3). Yes, they are equal.

tryIt.statement: Order $\frac{5}{6}$, $\frac{2}{3}$, and $\frac{3}{4}$ from smallest to largest.

steps.title: Give them the same-size pieces
steps.teach: Pie A is $\frac{2}{3}$ and Pie B is $\frac{3}{5}$ — but the slices are different sizes, so you can't just count them yet. Tap **"Give them the same-size pieces"** to re-split both pies into equal slices. Then count who has more shaded pieces.

steps.title: Why we find a common denominator
beats: Both pies got **re-split into 15 equal slices** — that's the **common denominator** ($3 \times 5 = 15$).
Pie A becomes $\frac{2}{3} = \frac{10}{15}$ (10 pieces shaded). The bar below shows those 10 out of 15 equal pieces.
Pie B becomes $\frac{3}{5} = \frac{9}{15}$ (9 pieces shaded). Same-size pieces now — just compare: **10 > 9**, so $\frac{2}{3} > \frac{3}{5}$.
This is the fraction twin of Ratios — **make the water equal** so you're comparing like with like.

steps.title: Compare two fractions
steps.intro: Find a common denominator, convert both fractions, then compare the numerators.

steps: The denominator 8 is already a multiple of 4, so use 8.

steps.title: Which fraction is bigger?
steps.intro: Find a common denominator and compare the numerators. Tap the correct answer.

problems.explanation: Common denominator 30: $\frac{5}{6}=\frac{25}{30}$ and $\frac{4}{5}=\frac{24}{30}$. Since $24<25$, $\frac{4}{5}$ is smaller.

steps.title: Compare these two
steps.explanation: Common denominator 18: $\frac{5}{6}=\frac{15}{18}$ and $\frac{7}{9}=\frac{14}{18}$. Since $15>14$, $\frac{5}{6}$ is larger.

steps.title: Fractions on a number line
steps.body: If you placed $\frac{2}{3}$ and $\frac{3}{5}$ on the same number line, $\frac{2}{3}$ ($\approx 0.667$) would sit just to the right of $\frac{3}{5}$ ($= 0.6$). The common-denominator method does exactly what the number line shows — and it works for any two fractions, no decimal needed.

steps.title: Try a different pair
steps.teach: Now try $\frac{3}{4}$ vs $\frac{5}{6}$. Tap to give them equal-size pieces and see who wins.

steps.title: Spotting equal fractions and ordering three
beats: Two fractions are **equal** when, after converting to a common denominator, their numerators match. $\frac{2}{3}=\frac{4}{6}$ because both become $\frac{4}{6}$.
To **order three fractions**, convert all three to the same denominator and line up the numerators smallest to largest.
The shortcut for finding a common denominator: multiply the two (or three) denominators together. You can always simplify later.

steps.title: Equal fractions and ordering
steps.intro: Convert to a common denominator, then compare or order.

steps: Multiply top and bottom of $\frac{2}{3}$ by 2: $\frac{4}{6}$.

examples.prompt: Order $\frac{2}{3}$, $\frac{1}{4}$, and $\frac{5}{6}$ from smallest to largest.
steps: Common denominator: 12 is the smallest number 3, 4, and 6 all divide into ($12\div3=4$, $12\div4=3$, $12\div6=2$), so use 12.

steps.title: Equal or ordered?
steps.intro: Convert to a common denominator, then decide. Tap the right answer.

problems.explanation: $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{3}{3} = \frac{9}{12}$. Same numerator and denominator, so they are equal.

problems.prompt: Which list puts $\frac{1}{2}$, $\frac{3}{4}$, and $\frac{1}{3}$ in order from **smallest to largest**?

steps.title: Spot the order
steps.prompt: Which correctly orders $\frac{2}{5}$, $\frac{1}{2}$, and $\frac{3}{10}$ from smallest to largest?

steps.title: What you learned
points: To compare fractions, **give them the same-size pieces** (common denominator), then count who has more shaded pieces — the larger numerator wins.
This is the fraction twin of **"make the water equal"** from Ratios, and it uses **Lesson 1** (multiply top and bottom by the same number to build an equivalent fraction).
Two fractions are **equal** when they have the same numerator after converting to a common denominator — like $\frac{2}{3}=\frac{4}{6}$.
To **order three (or more) fractions**, convert all of them to the same denominator and sort the numerators.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Two moves to lock in: comparing two fractions with a common denominator, and ordering three. The mixed set below has both — plus a sneaky equal pair. Tap a wrong choice and just try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.explanation: $\frac{2}{3}=\frac{4}{6}$ by multiplying top and bottom by 2.
Common denominator 20: $\frac{3}{4}=\frac{15}{20}$ and $\frac{7}{10}=\frac{14}{20}$. $14<15$, so $\frac{7}{10}$ is smaller.

problems.prompt: Order $\frac{5}{6}$, $\frac{2}{3}$, and $\frac{3}{4}$ from smallest to largest.

problems.explanation: $\frac{6}{10}$ reduces to $\frac{3}{5}$ (divide by 2). They are equal.

steps.title: You've finished "Comparing Fractions"
steps.body: Ancient Egyptian mathematicians wrote all fractions as sums of *unit fractions* (fractions with 1 on top, like $\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}$) — comparing them was so tricky they needed whole tables. You just did it in seconds with a common denominator. Next up: **adding and subtracting fractions** — and you already know the secret ingredient.

lessons.title: Adding & Subtracting Fractions
lessons.concreteComparison: You eat **1/4** of a pizza and your friend eats **1/2** of the same pizza. How much is gone? You cannot just add the 1 and 1 on top — the slices are different sizes. First you need **same-size pieces**, then you count them up. That one rule unlocks all fraction addition and subtraction.
lessons.objective: Add and subtract fractions with like and unlike denominators by finding a common denominator, and name an improper result as a mixed number.
concept: When denominators are **the same** (like denominators), the pieces are already the same size — just add or subtract the tops: $\frac{3}{8} + \frac{2}{8} = \frac{5}{8}$.

When denominators are **different** (unlike denominators), rename both fractions with a common denominator first — the same move as **equivalent fractions** (multiply top and bottom by the same number). Then add or subtract the tops.

If the result is an **improper fraction** (top ≥ bottom), convert it to a **mixed number**: divide the top by the bottom. For example $\frac{5}{4} = 1\frac{1}{4}$. This usually only shows up with unlike denominators when the sum is greater than 1.
lessons.keyIdea: You can only add or subtract fraction pieces that are **the same size** — same denominator. Make them match first (equivalent fractions), then add or subtract the tops.

facts.title: Like denominators
facts.explanation: Same-size pieces: just add (or subtract) the tops. The bottom stays the same.

facts.title: Unlike denominators
facts.latex: $\dfrac{a}{b} + \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{a\times d}{b\times d} + \dfrac{c\times b}{d\times b} = \dfrac{a\times d + c\times b}{b\times d}$
facts.explanation: Rewrite both fractions with the same denominator, then add the tops. Simplify the result if possible.

facts.title: Mixed numbers
facts.explanation: If the top is bigger than the bottom after adding, divide to get the whole-number part, and keep the remainder as the new top.

workedExamples.note: The denominators are already the same — pieces are the same size. Just add the tops.
Different denominators — make the pieces match first. The common denominator is 4.
workedExamples.solution: Rewrite $\frac{1}{2}$ as $\frac{2}{4}$ (multiply top and bottom by 2). Now add: $\frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{4} = \frac{3}{4}$.

workedExamples.note: Different denominators — find a common denominator. 6 works: rewrite 1/3 as 2/6.

commonMistakes.correction: The denominator tells you the **size** of each piece. When pieces are the same size, count only the pieces on top — the bottom stays: $\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{2}{4}$.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to rename both fractions: changing only one denominator and leaving the other as is.
commonMistakes.correction: Both fractions must be rewritten with the same denominator before you touch the tops.

commonMistakes.text: Leaving an improper fraction like $\frac{5}{4}$ without converting to a mixed number when a mixed number is expected.
commonMistakes.correction: Divide the top by the bottom: $5 \div 4 = 1$ remainder $1$, so $\frac{5}{4} = 1\frac{1}{4}$.

steps.title: Same pieces: just count the shaded ones
steps.teach: Both bars already show **eighths** — the pieces are the same size. Tap **Combine** to push them together and count the shaded pieces. You don't touch the 8 on the bottom at all.

steps.title: Why the bottom stays put
beats: The denominator is the **piece size**. Both bars cut into 8 pieces means every shaded piece is exactly $\frac{1}{8}$.
3 shaded pieces **plus** 2 more shaded pieces = **5** shaded pieces — and each is still $\frac{1}{8}$.
So $\frac{3}{8} + \frac{2}{8} = \frac{5}{8}$. The bottom never changes because the piece size never changed.
Same idea for subtraction: $\frac{7}{8} - \frac{3}{8}$ — unshade 3 pieces and count what's left: $\frac{4}{8} = \frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Adding and subtracting with like denominators
steps.intro: Same denominator? Add or subtract the tops. Leave the bottom alone.

steps: Same denominator (9) → pieces are the same size.

steps.title: Like denominators
steps.intro: Same bottom? Go straight to the tops. Tap your answer.

steps.title: Fractions in Mongolian cooking
steps.body: Traditional buuz dumplings are often made in batches. If you use $\frac{3}{8}$ of a bag of flour in the morning and $\frac{2}{8}$ in the evening, you've used $\frac{5}{8}$ of the bag total — the same pieces, just counted together. Mongolian cooks have been doing fraction addition for centuries without calling it that.

steps.title: Make the pieces match first
steps.teach: These bars have **different** piece sizes — you can't count them together yet. Tap **Match** to give them a common denominator (splits the left bar into equal-size pieces), then tap **Combine** to add. This is exactly the equivalent-fractions move from Lesson 1.

steps.title: The two-step: match, then combine
beats: **Step 1 — Match:** find a common denominator and rewrite both fractions. Multiply each fraction's top and bottom by the same number (equivalent fractions from Lesson 1).
**Step 2 — Combine:** now the pieces are the same size — add or subtract the tops, keep the bottom.
Example: $\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{2}$. Common denominator = 4. Rewrite $\frac{1}{2}$ as $\frac{2}{4}$. Then $\frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{4} = \frac{3}{4}$.
The common denominator is often the **larger denominator** if it's a multiple of the smaller — or multiply the two denominators together if no quick multiple shows up.

steps.title: Now try subtraction with unlike denominators
steps.teach: Same two steps — match first, then subtract. Tap **Match** to give both bars 6ths, then tap **Combine** to remove the shaded pieces.

steps.title: Unlike denominators — match then combine
steps.intro: Step 1: find a common denominator and rewrite. Step 2: add or subtract the tops.

steps.title: Unlike denominators
steps.intro: Match first, then combine. Tap your answer.

steps.title: When the answer goes over 1
beats: Sometimes adding two fractions gives a result bigger than 1 whole — you get an **improper fraction** (top ≥ bottom), like $\frac{5}{4}$.
Divide the top by the bottom: $5 \div 4 = 1$ remainder $1$. The whole-number part is $1$, the leftover is $\frac{1}{4}$.
So $\frac{5}{4} = 1\frac{1}{4}$ — read "one and one-quarter". This is a **mixed number**.

Example with unlike denominators: $\frac{2}{3} + \frac{3}{4}$. Common denominator 12 → $\frac{8}{12} + \frac{9}{12} = \frac{17}{12} = 1\frac{5}{12}$. In the interactive tool we always pick problems whose answer stays below 1, but you should know what to do when it doesn't!

fraction.label: the leftover 1/4 after taking out 1 whole

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Like denominators:** pieces already match — add or subtract the tops, keep the bottom.
**Unlike denominators:** use the equivalent-fractions move (multiply top and bottom by the same number) to give both fractions the same denominator, then add or subtract the tops.
**Mixed numbers:** if the result is an improper fraction (top ≥ bottom), divide top by bottom to find the whole-number part and the remainder fraction.
The common denominator is often the **larger** of the two denominators — check if the smaller one divides into it evenly.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: You've seen both kinds — like and unlike denominators — plus mixed-number results. This mixed set covers all three. If you tap a wrong answer, look at the explanation and try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Adding & Subtracting Fractions"
steps.body: Ancient Egyptian scribes around 1650 BCE used only **unit fractions** (fractions with 1 on top) to write every fraction — so they'd write $\frac{3}{4}$ as $\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4}$. Adding fractions with unlike denominators was central to their entire number system. You now know exactly how they did it.

lessons.title: Multiplying Fractions
lessons.concreteComparison: Imagine you have **$\frac{2}{3}$ of a chocolate bar** left — and you want to eat **$\frac{3}{4}$ of that piece**. How much of the *whole* bar do you end up with? Drawing a rectangle and shading two overlapping regions gives you the answer instantly: the dark overlap is $\frac{6}{12} = \frac{1}{2}$ of the bar. That is what multiplying fractions means — a fraction *of* a fraction.
lessons.objective: Find the product of two fractions using an area model, then use the shortcut (multiply tops, multiply bottoms, simplify) and apply it to real-world problems.
concept: **A fraction of a fraction is a product.** Shade $\frac{2}{3}$ of the columns of a grid and $\frac{3}{4}$ of the rows. The dark overlap — the part inside both shaded regions — is the product. It covers $6$ small squares out of $12$ total squares, so the answer is $\frac{6}{12}$.

**The shortcut:** multiply the two tops (numerators) to get the new top, and multiply the two bottoms (denominators) to get the new bottom. $\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{2 \times 3}{3 \times 4} = \frac{6}{12}$.
**Simplify the result** the same way you learned in *Equivalent Fractions*: divide the top and bottom by their greatest common factor. $\frac{6}{12}$ — both divide by 6 — gives $\frac{1}{2}$.
lessons.keyIdea: To multiply two fractions: multiply the tops, multiply the bottoms, then simplify. The area model shows *why* it works — the overlap squares out of the total squares.

facts.title: Multiplying fractions
facts.explanation: Multiply numerator by numerator and denominator by denominator, then simplify the result.

workedExamples.note: Multiply tops together and bottoms together, then simplify.
Multiply tops, multiply bottoms. Check if the result simplifies.
workedExamples.solution: $\dfrac{2}{5} \times \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{2 \times 1}{5 \times 3} = \dfrac{2}{15}$. Nothing divides 2 and 15, so $\dfrac{2}{15}$ is already simplest.

workedExamples.statement: A recipe uses $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of a bag of flour. You want to make $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of the recipe. How much of a bag do you need?

commonMistakes.text: Adding the denominators instead of multiplying them ($\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{5}$ — wrong!).
commonMistakes.correction: Multiplying fractions means **multiply** the tops and **multiply** the bottoms: $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{6}$.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to simplify the result (leaving $\frac{6}{12}$ instead of $\frac{1}{2}$).
commonMistakes.correction: Always check if the result simplifies — divide the top and bottom by their greatest common factor.

tryIt.statement: You have $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of a pizza. You eat $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of what is left. What fraction of the whole pizza did you eat?

steps.title: Build the overlap yourself
steps.teach: The grid has **3 columns** (for the thirds) and **4 rows** (for the quarters). The light shading shows $\frac{2}{3}$ of the columns; the stripe shading shows $\frac{3}{4}$ of the rows. The **dark overlap** is the product. Use the steppers to change how many columns and rows are shaded — watch the overlap fraction update.

steps.title: Counting overlap squares
beats: You have $\frac{2}{3}$ of a chocolate bar left. You eat $\frac{3}{4}$ of **that** piece. How much of the whole bar is that?
The grid has $3 \times 4 = 12$ small squares total — that's the new **denominator**.
The overlap has $2 \times 3 = 6$ dark squares — that's the new **numerator**.
So $\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4} = \frac{2 \times 3}{3 \times 4} = \frac{6}{12}$.
Simplify using what you learned in *Equivalent Fractions*: both divide by 6 → $\frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Seeing the overlap
steps.intro: Sketch the grid in your head: columns for one fraction, rows for the other. Count the overlap.

examples.prompt: Use an area model to find $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3}$.
steps: Shade 1 column and 2 rows. Overlap: $1 \times 2 = 2$ dark squares.

examples.prompt: Use an area model to find $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{3}$.
steps: Shade 3 columns and 1 row. Overlap: $3 \times 1 = 3$ dark squares.

steps.title: Read the overlap
steps.intro: Picture the grid: columns for one fraction, rows for the other. Count overlap squares ÷ total squares.

problems.prompt: What does the area model give for $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}$?
Area model for $\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{1}{4}$ — how many overlap squares out of how many total?
Area model for $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{4}{5}$ — what is the simplified answer?

steps.title: Name the overlap
steps.prompt: An area model has 5 columns and 2 rows. You shade 3 columns and 1 row. What fraction does the overlap represent?

steps.title: Area models are how builders think
steps.body: When an architect says a room is $\frac{3}{4}$ as wide as another and $\frac{2}{3}$ as long, they multiply those fractions to find the fraction of the floor area. Every blueprint calculation is a hidden area model.

steps.title: The shortcut — multiply tops, multiply bottoms
beats: The area model told us **why**: total squares = bottom × bottom, overlap squares = top × top.
**The shortcut:** $\dfrac{a}{b} \times \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{a \times c}{b \times d}$. Tops together, bottoms together.
Try it: $\dfrac{2}{5} \times \dfrac{1}{3} = \dfrac{2 \times 1}{5 \times 3} = \dfrac{2}{15}$. Nothing divides 2 and 15 — already simplest.
Always finish by simplifying, just like in *Equivalent Fractions* — divide top and bottom by the same number until you can't anymore.

steps.title: Shortcut matches the model
steps.teach: Try $\frac{2}{5} \times \frac{1}{3}$: set the steppers to 2 of 5 columns and 1 of 3 rows. The overlap is $\frac{2}{15}$ — and so is the shortcut $\frac{2 \times 1}{5 \times 3}$. They always agree.

steps.title: Using the shortcut
steps.intro: Multiply tops, multiply bottoms, then simplify with the equivalent-fractions technique.

examples.prompt: A garden plot is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of a field wide and $\dfrac{3}{5}$ of a field long. What fraction of the field does it cover?
steps: $\dfrac{6}{15}$. Both divide by 3 → $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of the field.

steps.title: Apply the shortcut
steps.intro: Multiply tops, multiply bottoms, simplify. Tap the right answer.

steps.title: Multiply and simplify
steps.explanation: Tops: $2 \times 1 = 2$. Bottoms: $5 \times 3 = 15$. Result: $\frac{2}{15}$. Nothing divides 2 and 15 — already simplest.

steps.title: Multiplying fractions is smaller
steps.body: When you multiply two fractions that are each less than 1, the answer is always **smaller** than either one — because you are taking a *part of a part*. $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}$. A quarter is smaller than a half. The number line gets shorter, not longer.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **A fraction of a fraction** = the dark overlap in an area model — overlap squares ÷ total squares.
**The shortcut:** $\dfrac{a}{b} \times \dfrac{c}{d} = \dfrac{a \times c}{b \times d}$ — multiply tops, multiply bottoms.
**Always simplify** the result — same move as *Equivalent Fractions*: divide top and bottom by their greatest common factor.
Multiplying two fractions less than 1 always gives a result **smaller** than either fraction — you're taking a part of a part.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Two skills locked in: reading the area model and using the shortcut. Work through this mixed set — if you tap wrong, just try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: An area model has 3 columns and 5 rows. 2 columns and 4 rows are shaded. What fraction is the overlap?
You have $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of a bottle of juice. You drink $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of what is there. What fraction of the full bottle did you drink?

steps.title: You have finished "Multiplying Fractions"
steps.body: You can now picture the overlap in an area model and use the shortcut to multiply any two fractions — then simplify the result using equivalent fractions. Next up: **dividing fractions**, where you will flip one fraction and multiply instead.

lessons.title: Dividing Fractions
lessons.concreteComparison: You have **$\frac{3}{4}$** of a chocolate bar and each friend gets a **$\frac{1}{8}$-sized piece**. How many friends can you feed? You're asking: *how many eighths fit inside three-quarters?* Count them up — the answer is **6**. That's fraction division: finding how many of one piece fit inside another.
lessons.objective: Divide a fraction by a fraction using the measurement model (how many fit?) and the keep-change-flip shortcut, and explain why the shortcut works.
concept: **Measurement model:** $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$ asks "how many $\frac{1}{8}$-size pieces fit inside $\frac{3}{4}$?" Count the pieces on a bar and you get 6.

**Keep-change-flip shortcut:** to divide by a fraction, *keep* the dividend, *change* $\div$ to $\times$, and *flip* the divisor to its reciprocal. So $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8} = \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{8}{1} = 6$.
**Why it works:** dividing by $\frac{1}{8}$ is the same as asking "how many eighths?", which is the same as multiplying by 8 — the reciprocal. Flipping is just the shortcut for that swap.
lessons.keyIdea: To divide by a fraction, **keep** the first fraction, **change** $\div$ to $\times$, and **flip** the second fraction (use its reciprocal). This works because dividing by $\frac{a}{b}$ is identical to multiplying by $\frac{b}{a}$.

facts.title: Keep-change-flip
facts.explanation: Flip the divisor (the fraction you're dividing by) to its reciprocal, then multiply. The answer is the number of divisor-size pieces that fit in the dividend.

workedExamples.statement: How many $\frac{1}{8}$-size pieces fit in $\frac{3}{4}$? In other words, find $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$.

workedExamples.note: How many sixths fit inside one-half?
workedExamples.solution: Keep $\frac{1}{2}$, flip $\frac{1}{6}$ to $\frac{6}{1}$: $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{1} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$. Three sixths fit in one-half.

commonMistakes.text: Flipping the wrong fraction — flipping the dividend instead of the divisor.
commonMistakes.correction: Only flip the fraction you are dividing **by** (the one after the $\div$ sign). Keep the first fraction exactly as it is.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to flip at all and just multiplying the two fractions straight across.
commonMistakes.correction: The divisor must become its reciprocal first. $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$ is $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{8}{1}$, not $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{8}$.

steps.title: How many fit?
steps.teach: This bar shows $\frac{3}{4}$. Tap **Count** and watch the tool mark off $\frac{1}{8}$-size pieces one by one. How many fit? **Count them** — that number is your answer.

steps.title: Why counting pieces gives the answer
beats: $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$ is asking: **how many $\frac{1}{8}$-size pieces fit inside $\frac{3}{4}$?**
Put both fractions over the same-size pieces (eighths): $\frac{3}{4} = \frac{6}{8}$. Six eighths fit inside six-eighths — so the answer is **6**.
This is the **measurement model** of division — same idea as asking how many 2-metre sticks fit in a 6-metre hall.
Tie-back to **equivalent fractions**: getting a common denominator is just the first step — it lets you count pieces on the same bar.

steps.title: Now try one-half divided by one-sixth
steps.teach: Bar shows $\frac{1}{2}$. Each piece is $\frac{1}{6}$. Tap **Count** — how many sixths fit inside one-half?

steps.title: Count how many fit
steps.intro: Re-write the dividend with the divisor's denominator, then count the pieces.

steps.title: Count the pieces
steps.intro: Convert the dividend to the divisor's denominator, then count. Tap your answer.

problems.explanation: $\frac{3}{4}$ already has fourths. Three $\frac{1}{4}$ pieces fit.

problems.prompt: $1 \div \frac{1}{3}$ — how many thirds fit in one whole?
problems.explanation: $1 = \frac{3}{3}$. Three $\frac{1}{3}$ pieces fit in one whole.

steps.title: Count the sixths
steps.explanation: $\frac{5}{6}$ already has sixths. Five $\frac{1}{6}$ pieces fit — so $\frac{5}{6} \div \frac{1}{6} = 5$.

steps.title: Cooks use fraction division every day
steps.body: A recipe uses $\frac{3}{4}$ of a cup of rice per person. You have a $\frac{1}{4}$-cup scoop. How many scoops? $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{4} = 3$ scoops. Every time a cook asks "how many scoops fill this amount?", they're doing fraction division — usually without even realising it.

steps.title: The shortcut: keep, change, flip
beats: Counting pieces works, but there is a faster shortcut for any fraction division.
**Keep** the first fraction exactly as it is. **Change** $\div$ to $\times$. **Flip** the second fraction upside-down (that flipped version is called the **reciprocal**).
Example: $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$ → $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{8}{1} = \frac{24}{4} = 6$. Same answer as counting!

**Why it works:** dividing by $\frac{1}{8}$ is the same as asking "how many eighths?", which is multiplying by 8. Flipping $\frac{1}{8}$ gives $\frac{8}{1} = 8$ — that's exactly the same move. Builds on **multiplying fractions** from the previous lesson.

steps.title: Keep-change-flip in action
steps.teach: Watch the shortcut animate: the bar marks off $\frac{1}{6}$-size pieces inside $\frac{2}{3}$. Confirm that $\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{6}{1} = 4$ matches the count.

steps.title: Keep-change-flip
steps.intro: Keep the dividend, change ÷ to ×, flip the divisor. Multiply straight across.

steps.title: Keep, change, flip
steps.intro: Apply the shortcut: keep the first, change to ×, flip the second. Tap your answer.

steps.title: The reciprocal always multiplies to 1
steps.body: In algebra the flipped version of a fraction is called its **reciprocal** (or "multiplicative inverse"). Every non-zero number has one: the reciprocal of $\frac{3}{4}$ is $\frac{4}{3}$, and $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{4}{3} = 1$. Multiplying a number by its reciprocal always gives 1 — that is the secret behind why keep-change-flip works.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Measurement model:** $\frac{a}{b} \div \frac{c}{d}$ asks how many $\frac{c}{d}$-size pieces fit inside $\frac{a}{b}$.
**Keep-change-flip:** keep the dividend, change $\div$ to $\times$, flip the divisor to its reciprocal.
**Why it works:** dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal — flipping is just that swap.
This builds directly on **multiplying fractions**: once you flip, it is just a multiplication problem.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Two ideas to lock in: the counting model and the keep-change-flip shortcut. Run this mixed set — every question has a whole-number answer so you can check your work easily.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Dividing Fractions"
steps.body: You can now see *why* dividing a fraction works (counting pieces) and *how* to do it fast (keep-change-flip). These two ideas together — the picture and the shortcut — are the full story. Next up: fraction word problems, where you'll decide which operation to use and then go.

lessons.title: Fraction Word Problems
lessons.concreteComparison: A recipe needs $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of butter and $\frac{1}{3}$ cup of sugar — how much is that **together**? A garden is $\frac{3}{4}$ of a field and you plant $\frac{2}{3}$ of that — how much did you plant? You have $\frac{3}{4}$ of a pizza and each friend gets $\frac{1}{8}$ — how many friends can you feed? Every real question about fractions is hiding one of four operations. This lesson teaches you to spot which one before you calculate.
lessons.objective: Read a fraction word problem, identify which operation (add, subtract, multiply, or divide) the story needs, solve it, and simplify the answer in context.
concept: The story tells you the operation. **"Together," "combined," "total"** signal addition. **"Left over," "difference," "how much more"** signal subtraction. **"of" between two fractions** (as in "$\frac{1}{2}$ of $\frac{2}{3}$") signals multiplication. **"How many times does X fit into Y," "cut into pieces of size..."** signals division.
For **add or subtract**, give the fractions the same-size pieces (common denominator) first — the same move from **Comparing Fractions** and **Adding Fractions**. Then combine or take away the tops.
**Multiply** means area: shade columns for the first fraction, rows for the second — the double-shaded overlap is the answer. The keyword is **"of"** placed between two fractions.
**Divide** means "how many fit?": flip the divisor and multiply, or think of it as fitting the smaller piece into the larger one. Always check: does the answer make sense in the story?
lessons.keyIdea: The story tells you the operation — then you use the fraction skills from Lessons 1–5. The only new skill here is **reading the story** and matching it to the right operation.

facts.title: Four operation signals
facts.explanation: Read the story first. Identify the key word or phrase, then reach for the matching operation.

facts.title: Add fractions (find common denominator)
facts.explanation: Give both fractions the same-size pieces (common denominator), then add the tops.

facts.title: Multiply fractions (area model)
facts.explanation: Multiply tops together and bottoms together. The area model shows why: columns times rows equals overlap.

facts.title: Divide fractions (flip and multiply)
facts.explanation: Flip the divisor and multiply. Alternatively: count how many of the second fraction fit in the first.

workedExamples.statement: A recipe uses $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of butter and $\frac{1}{3}$ cup of sugar. How much is that altogether?
workedExamples.note: "Altogether" signals addition. Give both fractions the same-size pieces first.
workedExamples.solution: Common denominator of 2 and 3 is 6. $\frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{6}$ and $\frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{6}$. So $\frac{3}{6} + \frac{2}{6} = \frac{5}{6}$ cup. **Answer: $\frac{5}{6}$ cup.**

workedExamples.statement: A garden covers $\frac{3}{4}$ of a field. You plant flowers in $\frac{2}{3}$ of the garden. What fraction of the whole field has flowers?
workedExamples.note: "$\frac{2}{3}$ **of** $\frac{3}{4}$" — the word "of" between two fractions means multiply.

workedExamples.statement: You have $\frac{3}{4}$ of a pizza. Each friend gets $\frac{1}{8}$ of the whole pizza. How many friends can you feed?
workedExamples.note: "How many $\frac{1}{8}$s fit in $\frac{3}{4}$?" — that is division.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying when the story calls for addition — e.g. confusing "together" with "of".
commonMistakes.correction: Underline the key word first. "Together" or "total" → add. "Of" between two fractions → multiply.

commonMistakes.text: Adding the tops and bottoms separately when adding fractions (e.g. $\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{2}{5}$).
commonMistakes.correction: Give them the same-size pieces first (common denominator), **then** add only the tops.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to simplify the answer — e.g. leaving $\frac{6}{12}$ instead of $\frac{1}{2}$.
commonMistakes.correction: Always reduce to simplest form before writing the final answer.

tryIt.statement: Mia walked $\frac{1}{2}$ km to school and $\frac{1}{4}$ km to the library. How far did she walk in total?
A loaf of bread is $\frac{5}{6}$ full. You eat $\frac{1}{3}$ of the whole loaf. How much is left?
tryIt.solution: Common denominator is 6. $\frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{6}$. $\frac{5}{6} - \frac{2}{6} = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}$ of the loaf.

tryIt.statement: A chocolate bar weighs $\frac{2}{3}$ kg. You eat $\frac{3}{4}$ of the bar. How many kilograms did you eat?
You have $\frac{2}{3}$ of a metre of ribbon. You want pieces that are each $\frac{1}{6}$ of a metre. How many pieces can you cut?

steps.title: The story tells you the operation
beats: Every fraction word problem hides one of four operations. Your first job is **reading for the signal word.**
**"Together," "total," "combined"** — the amounts join up — **addition**.
**"Left over," "how much more," "difference"** — an amount is removed — **subtraction**.
**"of"** placed between two fractions (e.g. "$\frac{1}{2}$ of $\frac{2}{3}$") — a fraction of a fraction — **multiplication**.
**"How many fit?," "cut into pieces of," "how many pieces"** — counting portions — **division**.

steps.title: Spot the operation
steps.prompt: A jug holds $\frac{1}{2}$ litre of juice. You pour in $\frac{1}{3}$ litre more. Which operation gives the **total** juice?
steps.explanation: "Total" and "pour in more" tell you the amounts join — that is addition. $\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{5}{6}$ litre.

steps.title: Butter + sugar: see the bars combine
steps.teach: A recipe needs $\frac{1}{2}$ cup of butter and $\frac{1}{3}$ cup of sugar. The bars show each amount — then they combine into same-size pieces. Watch how the common denominator snaps into place before the tops add.

steps.title: Same-size pieces, then add the tops
beats: You cannot add $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{1}{3}$ directly — the pieces are different sizes.
Give them the **same-size pieces** (common denominator = 6): $\frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{6}$, $\frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{6}$.
Now the pieces match — add the tops: $\frac{3}{6} + \frac{2}{6} = \frac{5}{6}$.
This is the same move from **Lesson 3 (Adding Fractions)** — just applied to a real story.

steps.title: Add and subtract in context
steps.intro: Spot the signal word. Give fractions the same-size pieces. Then combine or take away.

examples.prompt: Mia walked $\frac{1}{2}$ km to school and $\frac{1}{4}$ km to the library. How far in total?
A loaf of bread is $\frac{5}{6}$ full. You eat $\frac{1}{3}$ of the whole loaf. How much is left?

steps.title: Add and subtract: pick the right answer
steps.intro: Underline the signal word, match the operation, then compute.

problems.prompt: A tank is $\frac{7}{8}$ full of water. You use $\frac{3}{8}$ of a tank. How much is left?
Davi drinks $\frac{1}{3}$ litre of water in the morning and $\frac{1}{2}$ litre at lunch. How much altogether?
Biscuits need $\frac{2}{5}$ cup of oats and $\frac{3}{10}$ cup of flour. Total dry ingredients?

steps.title: Subtract in context
steps.prompt: A ribbon is $\frac{3}{4}$ m long. You cut off $\frac{1}{6}$ m. How much is left?

steps.title: Recipes run on fraction addition every day
steps.body: Professional bakers halve and double recipes constantly — adding $\frac{1}{2}$ cup to $\frac{1}{3}$ cup, subtracting what they already poured. Fraction addition word problems are literally what happens in every kitchen, every day.

steps.title: "of" means area: see the overlap
steps.teach: A garden covers $\frac{3}{4}$ of a field. You plant flowers in $\frac{2}{3}$ of **that** garden — so you need $\frac{2}{3}$ **of** $\frac{3}{4}$. The area model shades 3 columns out of 4 for the garden, then 2 rows out of 3 for the planted part. The double-shaded overlap is the answer.

steps.title: "of" → multiply tops, multiply bottoms
beats: When you see **"of"** between two fractions, multiply them.
$\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{3}{4}$: multiply tops → $2 \times 3 = 6$, multiply bottoms → $3 \times 4 = 12$, giving $\frac{6}{12}$.
Simplify: both 6 and 12 divide by 6, giving $\frac{1}{2}$.
This is the same move from **Lesson 4 (Multiplying Fractions)** — the area model you just saw is exactly why it works.

steps.title: Multiply fractions in context
steps.intro: Spot the word "of" between two fractions. Multiply tops, multiply bottoms, then simplify.

examples.prompt: A chocolate bar weighs $\frac{2}{3}$ kg. You eat $\frac{3}{4}$ of the bar. How many kilograms did you eat?
A water tank holds $\frac{5}{6}$ of a barrel. You use $\frac{3}{4}$ of the tank for irrigation. What fraction of a barrel did you use?

steps.title: Multiply in context
steps.intro: Look for the word "of" between two fractions. Multiply tops, multiply bottoms, simplify.

problems.prompt: A cookie recipe uses $\frac{3}{4}$ cup of flour. You make only $\frac{1}{3}$ of the recipe. How much flour?
A field is $\frac{3}{5}$ km long. You run $\frac{5}{6}$ of the length. How far do you run?

steps.title: "of" means multiply
steps.prompt: A jug holds $\frac{7}{8}$ litre. You pour out $\frac{1}{4}$ of the jug. How much do you pour?

steps.title: Cutting ribbon: how many pieces fit?
steps.teach: You have $\frac{3}{4}$ m of ribbon and want to cut pieces that are each $\frac{3}{8}$ m. How many pieces? The bar shows $\frac{3}{4}$ divided into $\frac{3}{8}$-sized chunks — count how many fit.

steps.title: Flip the divisor, then multiply
beats: "How many $\frac{3}{8}$s fit in $\frac{3}{4}$?" is a division question.
To divide by a fraction: **flip the second fraction (the divisor) and multiply.**
$\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{3}{8} = \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{8}{3} = \frac{24}{12} = 2$ pieces.
Always check: does the answer make sense? 2 pieces of $\frac{3}{8}$ m each $= \frac{6}{8} = \frac{3}{4}$ m. Correct.

steps.title: Divide fractions in context
steps.intro: Spot the division signal. Flip the second fraction, then multiply.

examples.prompt: You have $\frac{2}{3}$ litre of juice to pour into cups that each hold $\frac{1}{6}$ litre. How many cups?
A board is $\frac{5}{6}$ m long. You need pieces each $\frac{5}{12}$ m. How many pieces?

steps.title: Divide in context
steps.intro: Flip the divisor and multiply. Double-check that the answer makes sense.

problems.prompt: You have $\frac{3}{4}$ of a pizza. Each person gets $\frac{1}{8}$. How many people can you serve?
A rope is $\frac{3}{8}$ m. You cut pieces of $\frac{3}{16}$ m each. How many pieces?
You have $\frac{3}{4}$ m of wire. Each bracelet needs $\frac{1}{4}$ m. How many bracelets?

steps.title: Division in context
steps.prompt: You cut a board $\frac{7}{8}$ m long into pieces each $\frac{1}{4}$ m. How many whole pieces do you get?
steps.explanation: $\frac{7}{8} \div \frac{1}{4} = \frac{7}{8} \times 4 = \frac{28}{8} = \frac{7}{2} = 3\frac{1}{2}$ — so 3 whole pieces, with $\frac{1}{8}$ m left over.

steps.title: Fraction division powers real kitchens
steps.body: A chef has $\frac{3}{4}$ of a bag of rice and a serving is $\frac{1}{8}$ of a bag. How many servings? $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8} = 6$ — the exact same division you just practised. Fraction word problems are not made-up school math; they run every professional kitchen on Earth.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Read the story first.** Spot the signal: "together/total" → $+$, "left over/difference" → $-$, "of" between fractions → $\times$, "how many fit/pieces" → $\div$.
Addition and subtraction: give the fractions **same-size pieces** (common denominator) first, then combine or take away the tops.
Multiplication: multiply tops together and bottoms together — the **area model** shows why "of" becomes $\times$.
Division: **flip the divisor and multiply** — then check the answer makes sense in the story.
Always **simplify** before writing the final answer.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: This mixed set covers all four operations in story form — your job is to spot the signal word before you calculate. Tap a wrong choice and just try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything — spot the signal word, choose the operation, solve.

problems.prompt: Nina has $\frac{1}{4}$ cup of red paint and $\frac{5}{8}$ cup of blue paint. How much paint altogether?
A bucket is $\frac{5}{6}$ full of water. $\frac{1}{2}$ of a bucket leaks out. How much is left?
A plot of land is $\frac{3}{4}$ of a hectare. You plant crops on $\frac{1}{3}$ of the plot. What fraction of a hectare has crops?
You have $\frac{2}{3}$ kg of trail mix and divide it into bags of $\frac{1}{6}$ kg each. How many bags?
A jug holds $\frac{2}{3}$ litre and another holds $\frac{5}{6}$ litre. Together, how many litres?
You have $\frac{3}{4}$ m of ribbon and cut pieces of $\frac{1}{4}$ m each. How many pieces?

steps.title: You have finished "Fraction Word Problems"
steps.body: You can now read any fraction story, spot the signal word, choose the right operation, and solve it — pulling together everything from Lessons 1 through 5 into one powerful skill. You have completed the Fractions chapter. Next in Grade 6: **Decimals**, where fractions meet place value.

_lessonDraft_dividing-fractions.title: Dividing Fractions
_lessonDraft_dividing-fractions.concreteComparison: You have **$\frac{3}{4}$** of a chocolate bar and each friend gets a **$\frac{1}{8}$-sized piece**. How many friends can you feed? You're asking: *how many eighths fit inside three-quarters?* Count them up — the answer is **6**. That's fraction division: finding how many of one piece fit inside another.
_lessonDraft_dividing-fractions.objective: Divide a fraction by a fraction using the measurement model (how many fit?) and the keep-change-flip shortcut, and explain why the shortcut works.
concept: **Measurement model:** $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$ asks "how many $\frac{1}{8}$-size pieces fit inside $\frac{3}{4}$?" Count the pieces on a bar and you get 6.

**Keep-change-flip shortcut:** to divide by a fraction, *keep* the dividend, *change* $\div$ to $\times$, and *flip* the divisor to its reciprocal. So $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8} = \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{8}{1} = 6$.
**Why it works:** dividing by $\frac{1}{8}$ is the same as asking "how many eighths?", which is the same as multiplying by 8 — the reciprocal. Flipping is just the shortcut for that swap.
_lessonDraft_dividing-fractions.keyIdea: To divide by a fraction, **keep** the first fraction, **change** $\div$ to $\times$, and **flip** the second fraction (use its reciprocal). This works because dividing by $\frac{a}{b}$ is identical to multiplying by $\frac{b}{a}$.

facts.title: Keep-change-flip
facts.explanation: Flip the divisor (the fraction you're dividing by) to its reciprocal, then multiply. The answer is the number of divisor-size pieces that fit in the dividend.

workedExamples.statement: How many $\frac{1}{8}$-size pieces fit in $\frac{3}{4}$? In other words, find $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$.

workedExamples.note: How many sixths fit inside one-half?
workedExamples.solution: Keep $\frac{1}{2}$, flip $\frac{1}{6}$ to $\frac{6}{1}$: $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6}{1} = \frac{6}{2} = 3$. Three sixths fit in one-half.

commonMistakes.text: Flipping the wrong fraction — flipping the dividend instead of the divisor.
commonMistakes.correction: Only flip the fraction you are dividing **by** (the one after the $\div$ sign). Keep the first fraction exactly as it is.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to flip at all and just multiplying the two fractions straight across.
commonMistakes.correction: The divisor must become its reciprocal first. $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$ is $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{8}{1}$, not $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{1}{8}$.

steps.title: How many fit?
steps.teach: This bar shows $\frac{3}{4}$. Tap **Count** and watch the tool mark off $\frac{1}{8}$-size pieces one by one. How many fit? **Count them** — that number is your answer.

steps.title: Why counting pieces gives the answer
beats: $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$ is asking: **how many $\frac{1}{8}$-size pieces fit inside $\frac{3}{4}$?**
Put both fractions over the same-size pieces (eighths): $\frac{3}{4} = \frac{6}{8}$. Six eighths fit inside six-eighths — so the answer is **6**.
This is the **measurement model** of division — same idea as asking how many 2-metre sticks fit in a 6-metre hall.
Tie-back to **equivalent fractions**: getting a common denominator is just the first step — it lets you count pieces on the same bar.

steps.title: Now try one-half divided by one-sixth
steps.teach: Bar shows $\frac{1}{2}$. Each piece is $\frac{1}{6}$. Tap **Count** — how many sixths fit inside one-half?

steps.title: Count how many fit
steps.intro: Re-write the dividend with the divisor's denominator, then count the pieces.

steps.title: Count the pieces
steps.intro: Convert the dividend to the divisor's denominator, then count. Tap your answer.

problems.explanation: $\frac{3}{4}$ already has fourths. Three $\frac{1}{4}$ pieces fit.

problems.prompt: $1 \div \frac{1}{3}$ — how many thirds fit in one whole?
problems.explanation: $1 = \frac{3}{3}$. Three $\frac{1}{3}$ pieces fit in one whole.

steps.title: Cooks use fraction division every day
steps.body: A recipe uses $\frac{3}{4}$ of a cup of rice per person. You have a $\frac{1}{4}$-cup scoop. How many scoops? $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{4} = 3$ scoops. Every time a cook asks "how many scoops fill this amount?", they're doing fraction division — usually without even realising it.

steps.title: The shortcut: keep, change, flip
beats: Counting pieces works, but there is a faster shortcut for any fraction division.
**Keep** the first fraction exactly as it is. **Change** $\div$ to $\times$. **Flip** the second fraction upside-down (that flipped version is called the **reciprocal**).
Example: $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{8}$ → $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{8}{1} = \frac{24}{4} = 6$. Same answer as counting!

**Why it works:** dividing by $\frac{1}{8}$ is the same as asking "how many eighths?", which is multiplying by 8. Flipping $\frac{1}{8}$ gives $\frac{8}{1} = 8$ — that's exactly the same move. Builds on **multiplying fractions** from the previous lesson.

steps.title: Keep-change-flip in action
steps.teach: Watch the shortcut animate: the bar marks off $\frac{1}{6}$-size pieces inside $\frac{2}{3}$. Confirm that $\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{6}{1} = 4$ matches the count.

steps.title: Keep-change-flip
steps.intro: Keep the dividend, change ÷ to ×, flip the divisor. Multiply straight across.

steps.title: Keep, change, flip
steps.intro: Apply the shortcut: keep the first, change to ×, flip the second. Tap your answer.

steps.title: The reciprocal has its own name in Mongolian math
steps.body: In algebra the flipped version of a fraction is called its **reciprocal** (or "multiplicative inverse"). Every non-zero number has one: the reciprocal of $\frac{3}{4}$ is $\frac{4}{3}$, and $\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{4}{3} = 1$. Multiplying a number by its reciprocal always gives 1 — that is the secret behind why keep-change-flip works.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Measurement model:** $\frac{a}{b} \div \frac{c}{d}$ asks how many $\frac{c}{d}$-size pieces fit inside $\frac{a}{b}$.
**Keep-change-flip:** keep the dividend, change $\div$ to $\times$, flip the divisor to its reciprocal.
**Why it works:** dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal — flipping is just that swap.
This builds directly on **multiplying fractions**: once you flip, it is just a multiplication problem.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Two ideas to lock in: the counting model and the keep-change-flip shortcut. Run this mixed set — every question has a whole-number answer so you can check your work easily.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Dividing Fractions"
steps.body: You can now see *why* dividing a fraction works (counting pieces) and *how* to do it fast (keep-change-flip). These two ideas together — the picture and the shortcut — are the full story. Next up: fraction word problems, where you'll decide which operation to use and then go.
